axis(side=3,at=seq(0.5,levs-0.5,length.out=2)+levs,tcl=0.5,
  labels=rep("",2))
axis(side=3,at=levs/2+levs,tcl=-0.5,labels="1")
axis(side=4,at=seq(0.5,levs-0.5,length.out=2),tcl=0.5,
  labels=rep("",2))
axis(side=4,at=levs/2,tcl=-0.5,labels="0")
axis(side=4,at=seq(0.5,levs-0.5,length.out=2)+levs,tcl=0.5,
  labels=rep("",2))
axis(side=4,at=levs/2+levs,tcl=-0.5,labels="1",las=1)
legend(x=ncol(model)+20, y=0,
  legend=c(
    expression(q[c*","*a] == sigma[a]^2/(2*delta^2)),
    expression(q[c*","*b] == sigma[b]^2/(2*delta^2)),
    expression(paste(q[ab] == q[ba]))
  ),
  xpd=NA, pch=15, col=hcl.colors(n=3),
  cex=0.8, pt.cex=1.5, y.intersp=1.5, bty="n")
clip(0,2*levs,0,2*levs)
grid()
```

To compute the probability under this model using the discretized diffusion approximation, we begin by building a $k \cdot n \times k \cdot n$ matrix, for $k$ levels of our discrete trait and $n$ bins of discretization. The main super- and sub-diagonals of this matrix will be populated by the values $q_{c,i} = \sigma_{i}^2/(2\delta^2)$ for each $i$ through $k$ discrete-state-dependent stochastic diffusion rates, $\sigma_{i}^2$. Each non-diagonal $n \times n$ sub-matrix super- and sub-diagonals, by contrast, contain the discrete character transition rates between each of the $k$ levels of our discrete character. An illustration of this model structure is given in Figure \@ref(fig:fig11) for 40 bins of discretization, although for accurate model parameter estimation a much finer discretization is recommended. In Figure \@ref(fig:fig11), the macro-structure of this transition matrix consists of $k \times k =$ four quadrants: two (those along the diagonal) describing the evolution of the continuous character and two (off the diagonal) describing the transition process for the discrete trait. Each non-zero row and column of the matrix corresponds with one particular combination of a continuous character bin of width $\delta$ and discrete character state (Figure \@ref(fig:fig11)).

As for our bounded model, above, the probability density of our continuous trait data can be calculated as $f(\mathbf{x}) = p(\mathbf{x}')/(\delta^N)$, where $\mathbf{x}'$ once again contains the discretized values of our continuous trait $x$.

To examine the effectiveness of this method to simultaneously estimate the discrete state specific rates of continuous trait evolution, $\sigma_{a}^2$ and $\sigma_{b}^2$, and the rate of transition between discrete states, $q_{ab}$ and $q_{ba}$, we undertook a small simulation experiment.

First, we simulated forty 200-taxon stochastic pure-birth phylogenetic trees with total tree depths of 1.0. On each tree, we next generated a binary discrete character history by first drawing the transition rate between character levels, $q_{ab} = q_{ba}$, at random from a uniform distribution on the interval $[1, 10]$. This corresponds to quite a high rate of transition between character states, given our total tree depth, which was done on purpose because if the rate of transition is low, then our discrete character changes very seldom and the distinction between our new joint vs. the traditional two-step estimation methods becomes much less significant. Then, we simulated continuous trait evolution that depended on our discrete trait histories, with $\sigma_{a}^2$ drawn randomly from a uniform distribution on $[0.5, 2]$ and $\sigma_{b}^2$ drawn randomly on $[5, 20]$. This ensures that $\sigma_{b}^2 > \sigma_{a}^2$, though the magnitude of difference between the two rates varied widely across simulations.

(Readers might note that the *scale* of this simulation study is smaller than we've used for previous models. This is because estimation is inherently more computationally challenging for this model than for the others described to this point. The reason for this is largely beyond the scope of the present article, but pertains primarily to the difficulty of large matrix exponentiation. This problem will become even more substantive for the final two models of this article, to be discussed below.)

```{r, echo=FALSE, eval=FALSE}
set.seed(77)

## set simulation conditions
nsim<-40
N<-200
Q<-matrix(c(-1,1,1,-1),2,2,
  dimnames=list(c("a","b"),c("a","b")))
q<-runif(n=nsim,1,10)
sig2<-replicate(nsim,
  setNames(c(runif(n=1,0.5,2),runif(n=1,5,20)),
    c("a","b")),simplify=FALSE)
## simulate trees and data
trees<-pbtree(n=N,nsim=nsim,scale=1)
tt<-mapply(sim.history,tree=trees,
  Q=lapply(q,function(q,Q) q*Q,Q=Q),
  MoreArgs=list(message=FALSE),SIMPLIFY=FALSE)
y<-lapply(tt,getStates,type="tips")
x<-mapply(sim.rates,tree=tt,sig2=sig2,
  SIMPLIFY=FALSE)

## create list to populate
fits_mbm<-list()
## iterate over simulated data
for(i in 1:nsim){
  cat(paste("dataset #",i,": \n",sep=""))
  ## repeat if non-convergent or fails
  fits_mbm[[i]]<-list(opt_results=list(convergence=1))
  while(fits_mbm[[i]]$opt_results$convergence!=0){
    class(fits_mbm[[i]])<-"try-error"
    while(inherits(fits_mbm[[i]],"try-error")){
      fits_mbm[[i]]<-try(fitmultiBM(tree=trees[[i]],
        x=x[[i]],y=y[[i]],parallel=TRUE))
    }
    cat(paste("\tlog(L) = ",
      paste(round(logLik(fits_mbm[[i]]),3),
        collapse=" "),"\n",sep=""))
  }
}

mk_fits<-mapply(function(t,x) fitMk(t,x,
  lik.func="pruning",rand_start=TRUE),
  t=trees,x=y,SIMPLIFY=FALSE)

save(nsim,N,q,sig2,trees,x,y,mk_fits,fits_mbm,file="fig12.rda")
```
```{r fig12, echo=FALSE, fig.width=9, fig.height=3.75, fig.cap="Parameter estimation under a discrete character dependent multi-rate continuous trait evolution model obtained using the discrete diffusion approximation. a) Generating vs. estimated $\\sigma_{a}^2$. b) Generating vs. estimated $\\sigma_{b}^2$. The color gradient of points matches the generating transition rate between discrete character states, $q_{ab} = q_{ba}$, with lighter colors corresponding to higher transition rates for the discrete trait. Model parameter estimates were obtained using the \\textit{phytools} R package (Revell 2024). See main text for additional details.", fig.align="center", out.width = "90%"}
load("fig12.rda")
col<-hcl.colors(n=100)
ln_q<-c(log(q),log(1),log(10))
col<-col[round((ln_q-min(ln_q))/diff(range(ln_q))*99)+1]
col<-col[-(length(col)-c(0,1))]

layout(mat=matrix(c(4,4,3,1,2,3),2,3,byrow=TRUE),
  heights=c(0,4),widths=c(3.5,3.5,0.75))
par(mar=c(5.1,5.1,3.1,1.1))

sig2_gen<-t(sapply(sig2,function(x) x))
sig2_est<-t(sapply(fits_mbm,function(x) x$sigsq))

xylim<-range(c(0.2,5,sig2_gen[,1],sig2_est[,1]))
plot(sig2_gen[,1],sig2_est[,1],log="xy",
  xlim=xylim,ylim=xylim,
  xlab=expression(paste("true value of ",
    sigma[a]^2)),
  ylab=expression(paste("fitmultiBM estimate of ",
    sigma[a]^2)),pch=16,col=col,cex=1.6,las=1,
  cex.axis=0.8,bty="n",axes=FALSE)
axis(1,at=c(0.2,0.5,1,2,5),cex.axis=0.8)
axis(2,at=c(0.2,0.5,1,2,5),cex.axis=0.8)
grid()
lines(xylim,xylim)
mtext("a)",line=1,adj=0)
xylim<-range(c(sig2_gen[,2],sig2_est[,2]))
plot(sig2_gen[,2],sig2_est[,2],log="xy",
  xlim=xylim,ylim=xylim,
  xlab=expression(paste("true value of ",
    sigma[b]^2)),
  ylab=expression(paste("fitmultiBM estimate of ",
    sigma[b]^2)),pch=16,col=col,cex=1.6,las=1,
  cex.axis=0.8,bty="n")
grid()
lines(xylim,xylim)
mtext("b)",line=1,adj=0)

par(mar=c(0,0,0,0))
plot(NA,xlim=c(-1,1),ylim=c(-0.75,0.75),axes=FALSE,bty="n")
labels<-c(1,2,5,10)
at<--0.5+(log(labels)-min(ln_q))/(max(ln_q)-min(ln_q))
axis(2,at=at,labels=labels,pos=0.25,las=1,
  cex.axis=0.7)
segments(x0=0.25,y0=-0.5,x1=0.25,y1=0.5,lend=1)
segments(x0=rep(0.5,100),x1=rep(0.5,100),
  y0=seq(-0.5,0.49,by=0.01),y1=seq(-0.49,0.5,by=0.01),
  col=hcl.colors(n=100),lwd=8,lend=1)
text(x=-0.8,y=0,
  "discrete character transition rate (q)",srt=90)

#################
```

Having simulated our data in this way, we proceeded to fit our discrete-trait-dependent multi-rate Brownian motion model using the discrete approximation of @Boucher2016 and this article. Parameter estimates for $\sigma_{a}^2$ and $\sigma_{b}^2$ compared to their generating values are given in Figures \@ref(fig:fig12)a and \@ref(fig:fig12)b. In general, we found that this procedure resulted in unbiased and reasonably accurate estimates of both evolutionary rates in the model, $\sigma_{a}^2$ and $\sigma_{b}^2$ (Figures \@ref(fig:fig12)a, \@ref(fig:fig12)b).

```{r, echo=FALSE, eval=FALSE}
cor(q,est_q)
cor(q,mk_q)
```

We also elected to assess estimation of the rate of transition between discrete character levels in the fitted model, $q_{ab} = q_{ba}$. We found that estimation in the discrete state dependent model was unbiased (Figure \@ref(fig:fig13)a). When we compared joint estimation of $q_{ab} = q_{ba}$ to estimation using a standard M*k* model (Figure \@ref(fig:fig13)b), we also found that the former was slightly more accurate than the latter. In particular, across our 40 simulations, the correlation between estimated and generating values of $q_{ab} = q_{ba}$ was $r = 0.81$ using joint estimation via the discretized diffusion approximation, while $r = 0.74$ when $q_{ab} = q_{ba}$ estimates were obtained by fitting a standard M*k* model (Figures \@ref(fig:fig13)a, \@ref(fig:fig13)b).

```{r, echo=FALSE, eval=FALSE}
smaps<-lapply(mk_fits,simmap)

bm_sig2<-matrix(NA,nrow=nsim,ncol=2,
  dimnames=list(1:nsim,c("a","b")))

ncores<-min(nsim,detectCores()-2)
mc<-makeCluster(ncores,type="PSOCK")
registerDoParallel(cl=mc)

ss_fits<-foreach(i=1:length(trees))%dopar%{
  lapply(smaps[[i]],phytools::brownie.lite,
    x=x[[i]])
}

stopCluster(cl=mc)

for(i in 1:nsim){
  bm_sig2[i,]<-rowMeans(sapply(ss_fits[[i]],
    function(x) x$sig2.multiple[c("a","b")]))
}

save(nsim,q,smaps,ss_fits,bm_sig2,file="fig13.rda")
```
```{r fig13, echo=FALSE, fig.width=9, fig.height=7.5, fig.cap="Parameter estimation for joint vs. separate modeling of discrete and continuous character evolution, under a generating scenario of discrete state dependent multi-rate Brownian motion evolution of the continuous trait. a) Generating vs. estimated values for the transition rates between discrete character levels \\textit{a} and \\textit{b} of the discrete trait, $q_{ab} = q_{ba}$, obtained from the joint model. b) Generating vs. estimated $q_{ab} = q_{ba}$ obtained from a standard M\\textit{k} model. In these simulations joint modeling was slightly more accurate ($r = 0.81$ compared to $r = 0.74$). c) Generating vs. estimated $\\sigma_{b}^2/\\sigma_{a}^2$ from the joint model. d) Generating vs. estimated $\\sigma_{b}^2/\\sigma_{a}^2$ for a two-step procedure, in which 100 stochastic character maps of the discrete character were first obtained, and then a state-dependent continuous trait evolution model was fit to each one and then averaged. Separate modeling of discrete and continuous trait evolution can result in a substantive downward bias in $\\sigma_{b}^2/\\sigma_{a}^2$, as previously shown in Revell (2013). The color gradient of points corresponds to the generating transition rate between discrete character states, $q_{ab} = q_{ba}$, with lighter colors matching higher generating transition rates, as indicated in the right figure legend. Note that lighter points tend to correspond with greater downward bias in $\\sigma_{b}^2/\\sigma_{a}^2$ in panel (d), while no such trend is apparent under joint estimation (c). Model parameter estimates were obtained using the \\textit{phytools} R package (Revell 2024). See main text for more details.", fig.align="center", out.width = "90%"}
## Figure 14
load("fig12.rda")
load("fig13.rda")

col<-hcl.colors(n=100)
ln_q<-c(log(q),log(1),log(10))
col<-col[round((ln_q-min(ln_q))/diff(range(ln_q))*99)+1]
col<-col[-(length(col)-c(0,1))]

sig2_gen<-t(sapply(sig2,function(x) x))
sig2_est<-t(sapply(fits_mbm,function(x) x$sigsq))
layout(mat=matrix(c(1,2,5,3,4,5),2,3,byrow=TRUE),heights=c(3.5,3.5),
  widths=c(3.5,3.5,0.75))
par(mar=c(5.1,5.1,3.1,1.1))

est_q<-sapply(fits_mbm,function(x) x$rates)
mk_q<-sapply(mk_fits,function(x) x$rates)
xylim<-range(c(1,20,est_q,mk_q))
plot(q,est_q,log="xy",xlim=xylim,ylim=xylim,
  xlab="true value of q",
  ylab="fitmultiBM estimate of q",pch=16,col=col,
  cex=1.6,las=1,cex.axis=0.8,bty="n")
grid()
lines(xylim,xylim)
mtext("a)",line=1,adj=0)
plot(q,mk_q,log="xy",xlim=xylim,ylim=xylim,
  xlab="true value of q",
  ylab="fitMk estimate of q",pch=16,col=col,
  cex=1.6,las=1,cex.axis=0.8,bty="n")
grid()
lines(xylim,xylim)
mtext("b)",line=1,adj=0)

est_sigsq<-t(sapply(fits_mbm,function(x) x$sigsq))
xylim<-range(c(1,
  sig2_gen[,2]/sig2_gen[,1],
  est_sigsq[,2]/est_sigsq[,1],
  bm_sig2[,2]/bm_sig2[,1]))
plot(sig2_gen[,2]/sig2_gen[,1],
  est_sigsq[,2]/est_sigsq[,1],
  log="xy",xlim=xylim,ylim=xylim,
  xlab=expression(paste("generating ",
    sigma[b]^2/sigma[a]^2)),
  ylab=expression(paste("fitmultiBM estimated ",
    sigma[b]^2/sigma[a]^2)),
  pch=16,col=col,cex=1.6,las=1,
  cex.axis=0.8,bty="n")
grid()
lines(xylim,xylim)
mtext("c)",line=1,adj=0)
plot(sig2_gen[,2]/sig2_gen[,1],
  bm_sig2[,2]/bm_sig2[,1],
  log="xy",xlim=xylim,ylim=xylim,
  xlab=expression(paste("generating ",
    sigma[b]^2/sigma[a]^2)),
  ylab=expression(paste("brownie.lite estimated ",
    sigma[b]^2/sigma[a]^2)),
  pch=16,col=col,cex=1.6,las=1,
  cex.axis=0.8,bty="n")
grid()
lines(xylim,xylim)
mtext("d)",line=1,adj=0)

par(mar=c(0,0,0,0))
plot(NA,xlim=c(-1,1),ylim=c(-0.75,0.75),axes=FALSE,bty="n")
labels<-c(1,2,5,10)
at<--0.5+(log(labels)-min(ln_q))/(max(ln_q)-min(ln_q))
axis(2,at=at,labels=labels,pos=0.25,las=1,
  cex.axis=0.7)
segments(x0=0.25,y0=-0.5,x1=0.25,y1=0.5,lend=1)
segments(x0=rep(0.5,100),x1=rep(0.5,100),
  y0=seq(-0.5,0.49,by=0.01),y1=seq(-0.49,0.5,by=0.01),
  col=hcl.colors(n=100),lwd=8,lend=1)
text(x=-0.8,y=0,
  "discrete character transition rate (q)",srt=90)
```

In addition to this analysis, we also elected to compare estimation of $\sigma_{a}^2$ and $\sigma_{b}^2$ between jointly modeling discrete and continuous trait evolution, and the traditional two-step procedure of first undertaking stochastic character mapping following @Huelsenbeck2003, fitting a state-dependent rate heterogeneous trait evolution model to each stochastic character map using the model of @OMeara2006, and then averaging across stochastic maps [@Revell2022-book]. Rather than directly comparing parameter estimates themselves, we compared the correlation of the ratio of $\sigma_{b}^2/\sigma_{a}^2$ between generating values and their estimates via these two contrasting methods [@Revell2013b]. The results from this analysis are shown in Figures \@ref(fig:fig13)c and \@ref(fig:fig13)d. In general, we found that $\sigma_{b}^2/\sigma_{a}^2$ was nearly unbiased in our joint model fit using the discretized diffusion approximation across a range of generating $\sigma_{b}^2/\sigma_{a}^2$ varying from $<3$ to almost $40$ (Figure \@ref(fig:fig13)c). By contrast, estimated $\sigma_{b}^2/\sigma_{a}^2$ was strongly *downwardly* biased when obtained using the traditional two-step procedure (Figure \@ref(fig:fig13)d), particularly for higher transition rates of the discrete character, $q_{ab} = q_{ba}$. This reiterates the general finding of @Revell2013b.

## A continuous-character-dependent discrete trait evolution model

In addition to a model in which the state of a discrete character influences the stochastic diffusion of our continuous trait, which we saw in the previous section, the discrete approximation also permits us to quite easily imagine and fit the inverse model where the state of a continuous character influences the transition rate of a discrete trait. A biological scenario in which we might hypothesize that this model could be a good explanation for our data might include one where the rate of transition to a particular trait, like viviparity or live-birth in reptiles, is influenced by a continuously-valued environmental factor, such as temperature [e.g., @Shine1983; @LambertWiens2013; @PyronBurbrink2014]. Lots of other conceivable hypotheses of discrete phenotypic trait evolution could imply a similar relation. 

Figures \@ref(fig:fig14)c and \@ref(fig:fig14)d illustrate the general structure of this process in which we've supposed a sigmoidal relationship between the value of our continuous trait and the rates of substitution between the two levels of a continuous character dependent discrete trait, $a$ and $b$. This is an arbitrary decision, and our general approach should (in principle) work for any functional form for the relationship between our continuous character and the transition rates of the discrete trait. The sigmoid form is convenient because (depending on its parameterization) it can be designed to be positive everywhere and includes continuous character independence (i.e., a "flat" curve) as a special case. Nonetheless, it would be straightforward to adapt our implementation of this method to other functional forms. As far as we know, and like the semi-threshold model discussed in a previous section, this model is novel to the present article [but see @IvesGarland2010 for a statistical approach designed to investigate a similar underlying biological hypothesis].

In the scenario illustrated by the figure, the "forward" transition rate of our discrete character ($q_{ab}$) is high for low values of our continuous trait and low for higher values, while the converse is true with respect to the "backward" transition rate from $b$ to $a$, $q_{ba}$ (Figures \@ref(fig:fig14)c, \@ref(fig:fig14)d). Data for a continuous character and discrete trait simulated on a stochastic phylogeny with 400 tips under these conditions is illustrated by Figures \@ref(fig:fig14)a and \@ref(fig:fig14)b, respectively. In the figure, we see that the evolutionary process has predictably resulted in an association between relatively high values of our continuous character (Figure \@ref(fig:fig14)a) and condition $b$ of the discrete trait (Figure \@ref(fig:fig14)b), as well as between relatively low values of the continuous character (Figure \@ref(fig:fig14)a) and condition $a$ of the discrete trait (Figure \@ref(fig:fig14)b). This is one scenario of continuous character trait dependent discrete character evolution; however, our model would also be appropriate for conditions in which (for instance) one extreme of the continuous trait distribution was associated with higher backward and forward rates of the discrete character, and the other with low discrete trait rates. Though this evolutionary process unambiguously corresponds to continuous-character-dependent rate-varying discrete trait evolution, it would not tend to result in the same predictable character association shown by Figures \@ref(fig:fig14)a and \@ref(fig:fig14)b.

```{r, echo=FALSE, eval=FALSE}
rm(list=ls())
set.seed(99)
## number of taxa in my tree
N<-400
tree<-pbtree(n=N,scale=10)
## number of steps in simulation
nn<-400
## this creates a tree with many singleton
## nodes
h<-max(nodeHeights(tree))
tt<-map.to.singleton(
  make.era.map(tree,
    limits=seq(0,h-h/nn,length.out=nn))
)
## simulate Brownian evolution
y<-fastBM(tt,internal=TRUE,sig2=0.25)
## re-center to zero
y<-y-mean(y)
## compute the trait mean on each edge
edge_y<-rowMeans(
  matrix(y[tt$edge],nrow(tt$edge),
    ncol(tt$edge)))
## here's our sigmoid functions
q01<-2+(0.01-2)/(1+exp(-2*(edge_y-0.1)))
q10<-0.01+(2-0.01)/(1+exp(-2*(edge_y+0.1)))

x<-vector(length=Ntip(tt)+Nnode(tt))
x[Ntip(tt)+1]<-sample(1:2,1)
for(i in 1:nrow(tt$edge)){
  Q<-matrix(
    c(-q01[i],q01[i],q10[i],-q10[i]),2,2,
    byrow=TRUE)
  P<-expm::expm(Q*tt$edge.length[i])
  x[tt$edge[i,2]]<-sample(1:2,1,
    prob=P[x[tt$edge[i,1]],])
}
save(edge_y, h, i, N, nn, P, Q, q01, q10, tree, tt, x, y,
  file="fig14.rda")
```
```{r, echo=FALSE}
load("fig14.rda")
## continuous character
y<-y[1:Ntip(tree)]
## discrete trait
x<-setNames(letters[1:2][x[1:Ntip(tt)]],
  tt$tip.label)
rm(list=setdiff(ls(),c("tree","x","y")))
```
```{r, echo=FALSE, eval=FALSE}
fitcontMk_model<-fitcontMk(tree,x,y,parallel=TRUE,
  ncores=10,levs=100,maxit=5000)
fitcontMk_model
save(fitcontMk_model,file="fig16.rda")
```
```{r fig14, echo=FALSE, fig.width=8, fig.height=8, fig.cap="Illustration of the evolutionary process for a binary discrete trait whose rate of evolution depends on the value of a continuous trait. a) Evolutionary history of a continuous trait evolving by Brownian motion on the tree. b) Evolutionary history of a binary discrete character with two levels, $a$ and $b$, whose rates of transition between states depend (according to different functional forms in both directions) on the value of the continuous trait of panel (a). c) Generating (blue line) and estimated (black line) transition rate from discrete trait level $a$ to $b$, $q_{ab}$, as a function of the continuous character of panel (a). d) Generating (blue line) and estimated (black line) transition rate from trait level $b$ to $a$, $q_{ba}$, as a function of the continuous character value.", fig.align="center", out.width = "80%"}
load("fig14.rda")
## visualize them
layout(matrix(c(1,2,3,4),2,2,byrow=TRUE))
par(mar=c(1.1,4.1,3.1,1.1))
COLS<-hcl.colors(n=100)
cols<-COLS[round((edge_y-min(edge_y))/
    diff(range(edge_y))*99)+1]
plot(tt,edge.color=cols,edge.width=1,
  show.tip.label=FALSE,y.lim=c(-0.1*N,N))
mtext("a)",line=1,adj=0)
add.color.bar(0.75*max(nodeHeights(tree)),
  cols=COLS,title="continuous trait",
  lims=round(range(y),2),digits=2,
  prompt=FALSE,outline=FALSE,
  x=0.125*max(nodeHeights(tree)),y=-0.1*N,
  subtitle="",fsize=0.8)
COLS<-hcl.colors(n=2)
cols=COLS[x[tt$edge[,2]]]
par(mar=c(1.1,4.1,3.1,1.1),lend=1)
plot(tt,edge.color=cols,edge.width=1,
  show.tip.label=FALSE,direction="leftwards",
  y.lim=c(-0.1*N,N))
mtext("b)",line=1,adj=0)
legend(x=0.35*max(nodeHeights(tree)),y=-0.03*N,
  c("a","b"),lwd=4,col=COLS,horiz=TRUE,bty="n")
load("fig16.rda")
xx<-seq(-max(abs(y)),max(abs(y)),length.out=100)
q01<-2+(0.01-2)/(1+exp(-2*(xx-0.1)))
q10<-0.01+(2-0.01)/(1+exp(-2*(xx+0.1)))
par(lend=1)
YLIM<-c(0,max(c(q01,fitcontMk_model$q01,
  q10,fitcontMk_model$q10)))
YLIM<-c(0,2.6)
YLAB<-list(
  expression("transition rate (" * a %->% b * ")"),
  expression("transition rate (" * b %->% a * ")"))
obj<-plot(fitcontMk_model,add=TRUE,
  xlim=c(-max(abs(y)),max(abs(y))),ylim=YLIM,
  mar=c(5.1,4.1,3.1,1.1),ylab=YLAB)
par(mfg=obj$mfg[[1]])
par(usr=obj$usr[[1]])
lines(xx,q01,col=make.transparent("blue",0.5),lwd=5)
legend("topleft",legend=c("estimated","generating"),cex=0.7,
  lwd=c(1,5),col=c("black",make.transparent("blue",0.5)))
mtext("c)",line=1,adj=0)
par(mfg=obj$mfg[[2]])
par(usr=obj$usr[[2]])
lines(xx,q10,col=make.transparent("blue",0.5),lwd=5)
legend("topleft",legend=c("estimated","generating"),cex=0.7,
  lwd=c(1,5),col=c("black",make.transparent("blue",0.5)))
mtext("d)",line=1,adj=0)
```

To fit this model to data using the discretized diffusion approximation, we use a design matrix of our discretized space that is structurally similar to that of Figure \@ref(fig:fig11), but wherein the transition rates, $q_{ab}$ and $q_{ba}$, are allowed to vary as a continuous function of our quantitative trait, rather than assumed to take a constant value. As previously noted, in our implementation we suppose a sigmoidal functional form for this relationship, but there's no inherent necessity that it take this specific form. Additionally, and unlike the model design matrix of Figure \@ref(fig:fig11), here we assume a constant $\sigma^2$ for the continuous trait. This design matrix is illustrated in Figure \@ref(fig:fig15).

```{r fig15, echo=FALSE, fig.width=8, fig.height=6, fig.cap="Illustrative figure showing the design-matrix of continuous-character-dependent multi-rate discrete character evolution model for the discrete diffusion approximation. Here a specific sigmoidal form is being used to match the generating conditions of Figure 14, though in practice this form is precisely what would be estimated from the data. For visualization purposes only, the number of bins for discretization has been set to 40. In practice a much larger number would be used to obtain accurate parameter estimation and model selection. See main text for more details.", fig.align="center", out.width = "70%"}
## set number of levels for discretization
levs<-40
xx<-seq(-max(abs(y)),max(abs(y)),length.out=levs)
q01<-2+(0.01-2)/(1+exp(-2*(xx-0.1)))
q10<-0.01+(2-0.01)/(1+exp(-2*(xx+0.1)))
cols_q01<-round((q01-min(q01))/diff(range(q01))*99+1)
cols_q10<-round((q10-min(q10))/diff(range(q10))*99+1)
model<-matrix(0,ncol=2*levs,nrow=2*levs)
for(i in 1:(levs-1)){
  model[i,i+1]<-101
  model[i+1,i]<-101
  model[i+levs,i+1+levs]<-101
  model[i+1+levs,i+levs]<-101
}
for(i in 1:levs){
  model[i,i+levs]<-cols_q01[i]
  model[i+levs,i]<-cols_q10[i]
}
COLS<-c("white",hcl.colors(n=100),"grey")
par(mar=c(2.6,2.6,2.6,12.1))
plot(NA,xlim=c(0,ncol(model)+1),ylim=c(nrow(model),0),
  bty="n",axes=FALSE,xlab="",ylab="",asp=1)
for(i in 1:nrow(model)){
  for(j in 1:ncol(model)){
    polygon(x=c(-1,0,0,-1)+j,y=c(-1,-1,0,0)+i,
      col=COLS[model[j,i]+1],border="transparent")
  }
}
x_val<-c(xx[1],mean(xx),xx[levs])
axis(side=1,at=seq(0.5,levs-0.5,length.out=3),
  labels=rep("",3))
text(x=seq(0.5,levs-0.5,length.out=3),
  y=par()$usr[3]+2,labels=round(x_val,1),
  srt=45,adj=1,xpd=TRUE,cex=0.6)
axis(side=1,at=seq(0.5,levs-0.5,length.out=3)+levs,
  labels=rep("",3))
text(x=seq(0.5,levs-0.5,length.out=3)+levs,
  y=par()$usr[3]+2,labels=round(x_val,1),
  srt=-45,adj=0,xpd=TRUE,cex=0.6)
axis(side=2,at=seq(0.5,levs-0.5,length.out=3),
  labels=rep("",3))
text(x=par()$usr[1]-2,
  y=seq(0.5,levs-0.5,length.out=3),
  labels=round(x_val,1),
  srt=-45,adj=1,xpd=TRUE,cex=0.6)
axis(side=2, at=seq(0.5, levs-0.5, 
  length.out=3)+levs, labels=rep("",3))
text(x=par()$usr[1]-2,
  y=seq(0.5, levs-0.5, length.out=3)+levs,
  labels=round(x_val,1),
  srt=45, adj=1, xpd=TRUE, cex=0.6)
axis(side=3,at=seq(0.5,levs-0.5,length.out=2),tcl=0.5,
  labels=rep("",2))
axis(side=3,at=levs/2,tcl=-0.5,labels="a")
axis(side=3,at=seq(0.5,levs-0.5,length.out=2)+levs,tcl=0.5,
  labels=rep("",2))
axis(side=3,at=levs/2+levs,tcl=-0.5,labels="b")
axis(side=4,at=seq(0.5,levs-0.5,length.out=2),tcl=0.5,
  labels=rep("",2))
axis(side=4,at=levs/2,tcl=-0.5,labels="a",las=1)
axis(side=4,at=seq(0.5,levs-0.5,length.out=2)+levs,tcl=0.5,
  labels=rep("",2))
axis(side=4,at=levs/2+levs,tcl=-0.5,labels="b",las=1)
obj<-legend(x=1.08*ncol(model),y=0,
  legend=expression(q_c=sigma^2/(2*delta^2)),xpd=NA,
  pch=15,col="grey",cex=0.8,pt.cex=1.5,
  bty="n")
par(xpd=NA)
add.color.bar(0.5*levs,cols=hcl.colors(n=60),prompt=FALSE,x=2.2*levs,y=0.25*levs,
  title=expression(paste(q["ab"]," or ",q["ba"])),lims=NA,subtitle="",
  fsize=0.8,outline=FALSE)
```

Unfortunately, for computational reasons beyond the scope of the present study (but that once again pertain to large matrix exponentiation and that we hope to overcome via future research), it was not feasible for us to undertake even a modestly-sized simulation experiment to assess estimation under this model. We have nonetheless provided the maximum likelihood fitted model for the data and tree of Figures \@ref(fig:fig14)a and \@ref(fig:fig14)b in the solid black lines of Figures \@ref(fig:fig14)c and \@ref(fig:fig14)d. Note that all parameters of the backward and forward transition rate functional forms are being estimated from the data, the lower and upper asymptotes, the mid- or inflection point, and the curve steepness (Figure \@ref(fig:fig14)). At least in this single simulated example, the similarity to the generating conditions is notable (Figures \@ref(fig:fig14)c, \@ref(fig:fig14)d). Readers interested in employing this method for their own research are encouraged to download our simulation and analysis code (which is publicly available, see below) and undertake their own study of parameter estimation reliability for this model.

## A multi-trend directional model

As a final illustration of the power of our discretized diffusion approximation, we've chosen to develop another new model: a discrete-character-dependent multi-trend (i.e., multi-$\mu$), multi-rate (i.e., multi-$\sigma^2$), trended or directional Brownian motion model. This is a discrete-character-dependent stochastic diffusion model, but where diffusion has a linear trend ($\mu$) and in which both the diffusion rate ($\sigma^2$) and the trend ($\mu$) vary as a function of our discrete trait. This evolutionary scenario is illustrated via the simulation of Figure \@ref(fig:fig16). Note that we intentionally simulated a non-ultrametric tree because the trend model parameter $\mu$ and $x_0$ are mutually non-identifiable (that is, an $\infty$ number of $\mu$ and $x_0$ pairs result in the same likelihood) for ultrametric phylogenies [e.g., @HoAne2014; @Gill2017].

As far as we know, this specific model in phylogenetic comparative methods is novel to the present article; however, @Gill2017 provide a relaxed random walk model in which they also propose to jointly model continuous trait and nucleotide sequence evolution. [This is related to what we have done here, but assumes that the discrete characters and continuous trait evolution are conditionally independent, @Gill2017.] Among many possible applications, a biological scenario for this model might be one in which trended geographic diffusion of a host-switching wildlife virus is expected [e.g., @Gill2017], but where the trend direction switches as a function of the viral host, which in turn evolves via a continuous-time Markov chain.

```{r fig16, echo=FALSE, fig.width=8, fig.height=5, fig.cap="Illustration of the scenario of discrete-character-dependent multi-trend, multi-rate trended Brownian motion evolution. a) Simulated discrete character evolution of a binary character with two levels, $a$ and $b$, where $q_{ab} = q_{ba} = 0.5$. b) Continuous trait evolution under our model for $\\sigma_a^2 = 0.5$, $\\sigma_b^2 = 0.9$, $\\mu_a = 1.0$, and $\\mu_b = -0.7$. Under this scenario, the linear trend of our continuous trait is positive (i.e., increasing) when the discrete character is in condition $a$ and negative (decreasing) when in condition $b$.", fig.align="center", out.width = "80%"}
set.seed(99)
## simulate a tree
tree<-rtree(n=200)
h<-max(nodeHeights(tree))
tree$edge.length/h*10->tree$edge.length
simt<-map.to.singleton(make.era.map(tree,
    seq(0,max(nodeHeights(tree)),length.out=201)[1:200]))
## Q matrix for simulating discrete trait
Q<-matrix(c(-0.5,0.5,0.5,-0.5),2,2,
  dimnames=list(letters[1:2],letters[1:2]))
## simulated discrete trait history
simt<-sim.history(simt,Q,message=FALSE)
y<-as.factor(getStates(simt,"tips"))
par(mfrow=c(1,2),las=1,cex.axis=0.7,lend=1)
cols<-setNames(hcl.colors(n=2),letters[1:2])
plot(simt,colors=cols,ftype="off",
  mar=c(5.1,4.1,3.1,1.1),lwd=1)
axis(1,at=seq(0,10,by=2))
title(xlab="time")
legend("topright",c("a","b"),lwd=4,col=hcl.colors(n=2),
  bty="n",cex=0.8)
mtext("a)",line=1,adj=0)
sig2<-setNames(c(0.5,0.9),letters[1:2])
mu<-setNames(c(1.0,-0.7),letters[1:2])
par(mar=c(5.1,4.1,3.1,1.1),cex.axis=0.7)
x<-sim.multiTrend(simt,sig2=sig2,mu=mu,plot=TRUE,
  col=hcl.colors(n=2),lwd=1)
legend("topleft",
  c(expression(paste(sigma^2," = 0.5;  ",
    mu," = 1.0")),
    expression(paste(sigma^2," = 0.9;  ",
      mu," = -0.7"))),
  col=hcl.colors(n=2),lwd=4,cex=0.8,
  bty="n")
mtext("b)",line=1,adj=0)
```

To fit this model to data using the discretized diffusion approximation we take the same approach as for the preceding two models; however, here our diffusion process is asymmetric in the upward and downward directions. That is to say, $q_+ = \sigma^2/(2\delta^2)+\mu/(2\delta)$ and $q_- = \sigma^2/(2\delta^2)-\mu/(2\delta)$ (where $q_+$ and $q_-$ refer to the upward and downward transition rates between adjacent bins of our discretized continuous trait space for a given set of $\sigma^2$ and $\mu$ values) for diffusion rate $\sigma^2$ and linear trend parameter $\mu$, where $\delta$ is as previously defined [@Risken1996; @KushnerDupuis2001]. (Since both $q_+$ and $q_-$ must be $\ge 0$, fitting this model using the discretized diffusion approximation also requires that we select $\delta$ such that $|\mu| \leq \sigma^2/\delta$.)

To illustrate this model, we undertook a limited simulation study. We simulated twenty 200-taxon non-ultrametric trees of total length 10.0, and a binary discrete trait with $q_{ab} = q_{ba} = 0.5$, as in Figure \@ref(fig:fig16). We then sampled $\mu_a$ and $\mu_b$ randomly on $[0.2,1]$ and $[-1,-0.2]$, respectively. This way the trend, $\mu$, was always positive for discrete character level $a$ and negative for $b$. We randomly sampled $\sigma_a^2$ and $\sigma_b^2$ independently on $[0.3,1]$. For each tree and dataset we fit the multi-$\mu$, multi-$\sigma^2$ model using the discretized diffusion approximation.

```{r, echo=FALSE, eval=FALSE}
set.seed(99)
nsim<-20
levs<-100
mu_a<-round(runif(n=nsim,min=0.2,max=1),2)
mu_b<-round(runif(n=nsim,min=-1,max=-0.2),2)
sig2_a<-round(runif(n=nsim,min=0.3,max=1),2)
sig2_b<-round(runif(n=nsim,min=0.3,max=1),2)
fit_trend<-list()
for(i in 1:nsim){
  cat(paste("\nStarting iteration ",i,"....\n\n",sep=""))
  ## simulate a tree
  tree<-rtree(n=200)
  h<-max(nodeHeights(tree))
  tree$edge.length/h*10->tree$edge.length
  simt<-map.to.singleton(make.era.map(tree,
    seq(0,max(nodeHeights(tree)),length.out=201)[1:200]))
  ## Q matrix for simulating discrete trait
  Q<-matrix(c(-0.5,0.5,0.5,-0.5),2,2,
    dimnames=list(letters[1:2],letters[1:2]))
  ## simulated discrete trait history
  simt<-sim.history(simt,Q,message=FALSE)
  y<-as.factor(getStates(simt,"tips"))
  par(mfrow=c(1,2),las=1)
  cols<-setNames(hcl.colors(n=2),letters[1:2])
  plot(simt,colors=cols,ftype="off",
    mar=c(5.1,4.1,3.1,1.1))
  axis(1,at=seq(0,10,by=2))
  title(xlab="time")
  legend("topright",c("a","b"),lwd=2,col=hcl.colors(n=2),
    bty="n")
  mtext("a)",line=1,adj=0)
  sig2<-setNames(c(sig2_a[i],sig2_b[i]),letters[1:2])
  mu<-setNames(c(mu_a[i],mu_b[i]),letters[1:2])
  par(mar=c(5.1,4.1,3.1,1.1))
  x<-sim.multiTrend(simt,sig2=sig2,mu=mu,plot=TRUE,
    col=hcl.colors(n=2),lwd=2)
  legend("topleft",
    legend = c(
      bquote(sigma^2 == .(sig2_a[i]) * ";" ~~ mu == .(mu_a[i])),
      bquote(sigma^2 == .(sig2_b[i]) * ";" ~~ mu == .(mu_b[i]))
    ),
    col = hcl.colors(n = 2),
    lwd = 2,
    cex = 0.8,
    bty = "n"
  )
  mtext("b)",line=1,adj=0)
  fit_trend[[i]]<-fitmultiTrend(tree,x,y,parallel=TRUE,
    levs=levs,plot_model=FALSE)
  print(fit_trend[[i]])
  cat(paste("\nDone iteration ",i,".\n\n",sep=""))
save(mu_a,mu_b,sig2_a,sig2_b,fit_trend,file="fig17.rda")
}
```
```{r fig17, echo=FALSE, fig.width=8, fig.height=7.5, fig.cap="Parameter estimation under discrete character dependent multi-trend trended Brownian motion evolution. The discrete character was simulated with two levels, $a$ and $b$, for a constant transition rate between levels, $q_{ab} = q_{ba} = 0.5$. a) Generating vs. estimated $\\sigma_a^2$ under the model. b) Generating vs. estimated $\\sigma_b^2$. c) Generating vs. estimated $\\mu_a$, the linear trend parameter of the model when the discrete trait is in condition $a$. d) Generating vs. estimated $\\mu_b$. Model parameter estimates were obtained using the \\textit{phytools} R package (Revell 2024). See main text for additional details.", fig.align="center", out.width = "80%"}
load("fig17.rda")
est.sig2_a<-sapply(fit_trend,function(x) x$sigsq[1])
est.sig2_b<-sapply(fit_trend,function(x) x$sigsq[2])
est.mu_a<-sapply(fit_trend,function(x) x$mu[1])
est.mu_b<-sapply(fit_trend,function(x) x$mu[2])
par(mar=c(5.1,5.1,3.1,1.1))
layout(mat=matrix(c(1,2,3,4),2,2,byrow=TRUE),heights=c(3.5,3.5))
xylim.sig2<-c(
  min(c(sig2_a,est.sig2_a,sig2_b,est.sig2_b)),
  max(c(sig2_a,est.sig2_a,sig2_b,est.sig2_b)))
plot(NA,xlab=expression(paste("generating ",sigma[a]^2)),
  ylab=expression(paste("estimated ",sigma[a]^2)),
  bty="n",las=1,cex.axis=0.8,xlim=xylim.sig2,ylim=xylim.sig2)
mtext("a)",line=1,adj=0)
grid()
points(sig2_a,est.sig2_a,
  pch=16,col=cols[1],las=1,,cex=1.2)
legend("topleft","1:1 line",lwd=1,bty="n")
lines(xylim.sig2,xylim.sig2)
plot(NA,xlab=expression(paste("generating ",sigma[b]^2)),
  ylab=expression(paste("estimated ",sigma[b]^2)),
  bty="n",las=1,cex.axis=0.8,xlim=xylim.sig2,ylim=xylim.sig2)
mtext("b)",line=1,adj=0)
grid()
points(sig2_b,est.sig2_b,
  pch=16,col=cols[1],las=1,,cex=1.2)
legend("topleft","1:1 line",lwd=1,bty="n")
lines(xylim.sig2,xylim.sig2)
xylim.mu_a<-c(
  min(c(mu_a,est.mu_a)),
  max(c(mu_a,est.mu_a)))
plot(NA,xlab=expression(paste("generating ",mu[a])),
  ylab=expression(paste("estimated ",mu[a])),
  bty="n",las=1,cex.axis=0.8,xlim=xylim.mu_a,ylim=xylim.mu_a)
mtext("c)",line=1,adj=0)
grid()
points(mu_a,est.mu_a,
  pch=16,col=cols[1],las=1,,cex=1.2)
legend("topleft","1:1 line",lwd=1,bty="n")
lines(xylim.mu_a,xylim.mu_a)
xylim.mu_b<-c(
  min(c(mu_b,est.mu_b)),
  max(c(mu_b,est.mu_b)))
plot(NA,xlab=expression(paste("generating ",mu[b])),
  ylab=expression(paste("estimated ",mu[b])),
  bty="n",las=1,cex.axis=0.8,xlim=xylim.mu_b,ylim=xylim.mu_b)
mtext("d)",line=1,adj=0)
grid()
points(mu_b,est.mu_b,
  pch=16,col=cols[1],las=1,cex=1.2)
legend("topleft","1:1 line",lwd=1,bty="n")
lines(xylim.mu_b,xylim.mu_b)
```

In general, we found reliable estimation of the simulated discrete character dependent stochastic diffusion rates ($\sigma_a^2$ and $\sigma_b^2$) and linear trend parameters ($\mu_a$ and $\mu_b$) in this model (Figure \@ref(fig:fig17)), though many more simulations would be required to truly understand its properties.

```{r, echo=FALSE, eval=FALSE}
mk.q<-vector()
for(i in 1:nsim){
  tree<-fit_trend[[i]]$mk_fit$tree
  Data<-fit_trend[[i]]$mk_fit$data
  y<-vector()
  for(j in 1:nrow(Data)){
    y[j]<-colnames(Data)[which(Data[j,]==1)]
  }
  y<-sapply(strsplit(y,","),function(x) x[1])
  names(y)<-rownames(Data)
  mk.q[i]<-fitMk(tree,y,model="ER")$rates
}

est.q<-sapply(fit_trend,function(x) x$rates)
est.q
plot(est.q,mk.q)
mean(est.q)
mean(mk.q)
sqrt(var(est.q))
sqrt(var(mk.q))
```

We also checked parameter estimation for the rate of transition of the discrete trait in the model, $q_{ab} = q_{ba}$. We found no difference between $\hat{q}$ in our fitted trend models ($\bar{q} = 0.47$ compared to a constant true value of $q = 0.5$) and a standard M*k* model ($\bar{q} = 0.48$), although the former had a slightly lower variance across simulations than the latter ($\sigma = 0.076$ for joint estimation compared to $0.10$ for the standard M*k* model). Given the very small scope of simulations this result should be considered very preliminary; however, it comports with the finding of greater accuracy in the estimation of $q_{ab} = q_{ba}$ we reported for the discrete-character-dependent rate-varying Brownian model earlier in this article (Figure \@ref(fig:fig13)).

# Software implementation and data availability

All of the methods of this article are implemented in the R statistical computing software [@RTeam2025] package *phytools* [@Revell2012; @Revell2024]. All analyses of this article were conducted using *phytools*, *geiger* [@Pennell2014], and *ape* [@Paradis2019]. *phytools* and *geiger* also in turn depend on the core R phylogenetics packages *ape* [@Paradis2019] and *phangorn* [@Schliep2011]. *phytools*, *geiger*, *ape*, and *phangorn* are all publicly available through the Comprehensive R Archive Network, CRAN ([https://cran.r-project.org/](https://cran.r-project.org/)). All data and code of this article can be reviewed via a public GitHub repository ([https://github.com/liamrevell/Revell_etal.unlocking_pcms](https://github.com/liamrevell/Revell_etal.unlocking_pcms)).

# Concluding thoughts

A decade ago @Boucher2016 published an article that one of us (LR) has been consistently asserting since 2024 is "the most important phylogenetic comparative methods paper you probably haven't read." This is at least partly because LR had either not read, or read and not properly appreciated, this article at the time it was written nor for several years afterwards. Though certainly many others read the article, it's easy to argue that it was underappreciated by the field since it has been cited a mere 55 times since its publication. [For reference, this tally almost exactly matches the number of times an LR lab undergraduate-authored paper on lizard tail autotomy in the *Journal of Herpetology* from the same year has been cited as of the time of writing, @Tyler2016.]

The @Boucher2016 article introduced a new model, bounded Brownian motion, and at the same time circumvented the intractability of the likelihood function for reasonably-sized trees by devising a clever procedure for approximating the likelihood using what we've referred to herein as the discretized diffusion approximation. In essence, this approach replaces the underlying diffusion process with a continuous-time Markov chain defined on a discretized trait space with transition rates between adjacent bins chosen so that the chain approximates the generator of the diffusion process. This sort of construction is closely-related to classical finite-difference approximations of diffusion widely-employed throughout the stochastic process literature [e.g., @KushnerDupuis2001; @Gardiner2009; @Pavliotis2014]. What was truly novel about @Boucher2016, then, was not the general mathematical idea, but its application to phylogenetic comparative models. As discussed in the Introduction, a subsequent contribution by some of the same authors, @Boucher2018, extended this latter innovation to a wider variety of continuous trait evolution models; however, in our opinion, this article is also underrecognized.

In 2024, two of the other authors of the present article (MA and LH) invited the lead author (LR) to participate in a study they were working on (with author KM and others) on the topic of "circular" Brownian motion [@Martinet2026], also using the technique of @Boucher2016. At that time, it became apparent to LR that there might be a substantially larger number of other applications of this estimation procedure than those that had already been identified. This was the origin of the present article.

Herein we've presented (as far as we can ascertain from the literature) at least three or four totally novel models for the phylogenetic comparative analysis of discrete and/or continuous trait evolution. Nonetheless, we also confidently assert that this barely scratches the surface of the broad range of potential uses of this powerful approach. 

Some additional applications that we have not covered include a wide variety of so-called "hidden-rate" models [@Beaulieu2013; @Boyko2021]. In a hidden-rate model, we might study discrete character dependent continuous trait evolution, but where the discrete character trait is unobserved, thus allowing the continuous trait data to entirely dictate the phylogenetic position of shifts between rates or evolutionary regimes. Indeed, LR has already implemented precisely this model (in a few flavors) within his R package *phytools* [@Revell2024]. Similarly, it's straightforward to extend the same logic to our continuous character dependent rate varying discrete trait model, though, at the time of writing, this had not yet been implemented in software. As opposed to bounded evolution of @Boucher2016 or the semi-threshold model of the present article, we also might imagine evolutionary limits in which the bounds were absorbing or "sticky." This model, too, is straightforward to implement using the discretized diffusion approximation and is already available to both simulate and fit to data in *phytools* [@Revell2024]. We've focused exclusively on "gradual" continuous character models, but the discretized diffusion approximation would easily extend to process models with discontinuous jumps. For example, it would be very easy to imagine and implement in software a model of continuous character evolution but where a trait is "turned off" or lost in a single evolutionary event. 

We believe that both what we've covered in this article and mentioned above could represent but the tip of a much larger iceberg's worth of evolutionary scenarios for which likelihood functions are intractable but where the discretized diffusion approximation may be suitable. As such, we fully expect to continue discovering new applications of this approach into the future.

The famous @Maslow1966 law of the instrument states "if all you have is a hammer, everything looks like a nail" and is meant as a cautionary tale. The implication is that if you have but a single tool at your disposal, a cognitive distortion can arise in which many different problems will begin to look as if they can be remedied using this instrument. What we seem to have discovered in exploring the broad usefulness of the @Boucher2016 discretized diffusion approximation for the phylogenetic analysis of discrete and continuous trait data is a sort of inverse law of the instrument. If you look closely enough at many hypotheses and problems in phylogenetic comparative research that pertain to trait evolution, many do, in fact, turn out to be nails.

# Acknowledgments

This project was funded in part by a Tufts IRACDA Partner Faculty Research Award Program grant (NIH-NIGMS award K12GM133314, sub-awarded to LR) and by a Proposal Development Grant from the University of Massachusetts Boston (to LR and NH). We used ChatGPT (OpenAI) in grammatical and typographical proofreading, to check and improve technical clarity, and to assist in literature review; however, no portion of this article was written by AI and all content was reviewed and verified by the authors.

# References
